coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of biodegradable sodium alginate-based carbon dot-nanomagnetic composite (SA-FOCD) for enhanced water remediation using ANN modelling, RSM optimization, and economic analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Momina, Momina
Ahmad, Kafeel
coredata.dc:description: This study involves the synthesis of a magnetic‑sodium alginate bio-composite embedded with carbon dots, designed to eliminate pollutants like dyes and metal ions and tackle environmental issues. The modified particles are effectively incorporated into the biopolymers for improved adsorption and regeneration performance using an economically viable and environmentally sustainable process. The composite's surface morphology and chemical structure have been extensively characterized through various analytical techniques. It has been found that CD-modified nanoparticles demonstrate good dispersion, abundance in functional groups, and excellent adsorption performance. The adsorption process variables have been optimized using Response Surface Methodology (RSM), resulting in a maximum adsorption capacity of 232.44 mg/g achieved under optimal conditions. An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model with a topology of 3-5-5-1 is constructed to predict the adsorption capacity of composite, exhibiting superior predictive performance. The statistical physical model was also performed to understand the adsorption mechanism and orientation of dye molecules attached to the surface of the composite. The adsorption capacity using statistical physical method was found to be 467.57 mg/g. The composite exhibits good adsorption and regeneration performance in the column adsorption study. Furthermore, a detailed cost analysis of the synthesized composite was performed, ensuring its economic viability in real-world applications.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of biodegradable sodium alginate-based carbon dot-nanomagnetic composite (SA-FOCD) for enhanced water remediation using ANN modelling, RSM optimization, and economic analysis

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Civil Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi-110025, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Civil Engineering, Jamia Millia Islamia, New Delhi-110025, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study involves the synthesis of a magnetic‑sodium alginate bio-composite embedded with carbon dots, designed to eliminate pollutants like dyes and metal ions and tackle environmental issues. The modified particles are effectively incorporated into the biopolymers for improved adsorption and regeneration performance using an economically viable and environmentally sustainable process. The composite's surface morphology and chemical structure have been extensively characterized through various analytical techniques. It has been found that CD-modified nanoparticles demonstrate good dispersion, abundance in functional groups, and excellent adsorption performance. The adsorption process variables have been optimized using Response Surface Methodology (RSM), resulting in a maximum adsorption capacity of 232.44 mg/g achieved under optimal conditions. An Artificial Neural Network (ANN) model with a topology of 3-5-5-1 is constructed to predict the adsorption capacity of composite, exhibiting superior predictive performance. The statistical physical model was also performed to understand the adsorption mechanism and orientation of dye molecules attached to the surface of the composite. The adsorption capacity using statistical physical method was found to be 467.57 mg/g. The composite exhibits good adsorption and regeneration performance in the column adsorption study. Furthermore, a detailed cost analysis of the synthesized composite was performed, ensuring its economic viability in real-world applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past century, the exponential rise in population and accelerated industrialization has led to the emission of diverse pollutants into the environment, including dyes. Dyes, synthetic compounds extensively utilized in industries like textile, paper, and plastics, contribute to high dye concentrations in water. Disposing of such wastewater into water sources deteriorates water quality, harming aquatic life, human health, and water aesthetics [1,2]. Additionally, the improper disposal of dye and metal ions containing wastewater poses a significant challenge in treatment and remediation, posing economic and environmental burdens. Removal of synthetic dye compounds and metal ions from water has been extensively explored, with various methods such as photocatalysis, oxidation, filtration, coagulation-flocculation, ozonation, and adsorption processes [3]. Among these, adsorption is an efficient technique due to its simplicity and potential for adsorbent recovery and reuse. Dye molecules can bind to adsorbent surfaces via physical or chemical bonds. As the effectiveness of adsorptive separation techniques hinges directly on the quality and efficacy of the adsorbent, numerous exceptional adsorbents have been formulated for practical commercial usage. However, most of these adsorbents either need to be more effective due to diffusion limitations or lack sufficient active surface sites, making them challenging to separate from wastewater and consequently increasing their cost [4]. Moreover, the synthesized adsorbents are neither cost-effective nor biodegradable.

Thus, biopolymer-based adsorbents (Sodium alginate-based adsorbents) have gained prominence in water purification endeavors. In recent times, extensive research has explored the utilization of biopolymers as adsorbents owing to their facile synthesis, ecological compatibility, biodegradability, and cost-effectiveness [2,5]. The challenge in developing biopolymer-based nanocomposites lies in harnessing the full potential of natural constituents, separation from wastewater, and regeneration to ensure sustainability and ecological compatibility [6]. In light of environmental considerations and the imperative to diminish carbon footprint, attention is directed towards incorporating magnetic nanoparticles as an environmentally friendly filler in biopolymer-based nanocomposites. Due to their extensive surface area and diverse functionalization capabilities, nanoparticles possess a heightened quantity of active sites with facile magnetic separation, facilitating the effective elimination of synthetic dyes from water [7]. While numerous methodologies have been devised for fabricating magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) like Fe3O4, a substantial portion of these approaches necessitate the utilization of organic solvents and reducing agents. This escalates the product costs and engenders the potential for secondary environmental pollution. Thus, green synthetic routes for magnetic nanoparticles will overcome these limitations [8]. One more challenge observed is the agglomeration tendencies of magnetic nanoparticles while integrating with biopolymers, which can diminish interface volume and consequently reduce performance [9]. To tackle the issue of agglomeration and enhance the dispersion of nano-fillers within the polymer matrix, applying grafting or coating techniques involving functional groups such as -OH and COOH can elevate the dispersion quality and strengthen the bond with the biopolymer [10]. In this regard, carbon dots have been introduced in our study as a coating to provide a uniform dispersion of nanoparticles in the biopolymer matrix. Carbon dots (CDs) are a zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterial characterized by remarkable photoluminescence attributes, a wealth of π-domains, ample functional groups, environmental friendliness, substantial surface area, cost-effectiveness, and a straightforward synthesis methodology [11]. The characteristics exhibited by CDs render them compatible for interacting with diverse organic pollutants present in water, thereby presenting promising prospects for their utilization in wastewater remediation. Few research studies show that CDs establish covalent bonding or intermolecular interactions after impregnation with nanoparticles or biopolymers [12]. In this study, CDs have been prepared by apple peel and coated with green nanofiller (magnetic nanoparticles). The combination of CDs with nanofiller is further mixed with biopolymer to form a ternary bio nanocomposite and utilized for the adsorption of synthetic dyes.

The utilization of artificial neural networks (ANN) in adsorption processes has proven successful, allowing accurate forecasting of adsorbate removal efficiency and adsorption capacity through the utilization of input parameters used in experimental adsorption studies. In complex and nonlinear challenges such as chemical modelling, ANNs stand out as a more advanced choice than conventional linear models [13,14]. Due to its inherent qualities of simplicity, robustness, reliability, and ability to capture nonlinear relationships, ANNs offer a versatile approach to address and simulate a broad spectrum of complex engineering systems [15,16]. ANNs are a parallel distributed processing methodology employing neurons and weights to learn intricate connections between input and output variables. Neurons act as individual processing units interconnected with neurons in subsequent layers, giving rise to various configurations of ANNs [17]. ANN models have been applied extensively to predict the performance of adsorbents for the removal of various pollutants [18]. In contrast, Response Surface Methodology (RSM) is a powerful statistical and mathematical tool used to optimize and model complex processes, including those in adsorption. It provides the interplay between experimental variables and multiple responses. This relationship is established by considering effective empirical parameters that account for individual effects and interactions [19]. RSM can achieve the optimum results by minimizing or maximizing the response to predict the outcomes of adsorption process [20].

The novelty of this work is the impregnation of CDs-coated magnetic nanoparticles over sodium alginate biopolymer (SA-FOCD) to reduce the agglomeration issues of nanoparticles over polymer matrices. Moreover, the composite has been synthesized by biomass-based products, including CDs and magnetic nanoparticles, to reduce the overall cost of adsorbent and to ensure compatibility and biodegradability. Thus, the objectives of this study are (a) to synthesize ternary composite using CDs, Fe3O4, and SA, (b) to characterize the synthesized adsorbent by several methods to understand the chemical, physical, thermal, and optical properties, (c) to use bio-nanocomposite for removal of synthetic dye from aqueous solution followed by continuous adsorption study, (d) to implement the RSM and ANN for determining the optimal conditions and prediction of adsorption process, and (e) to perform cost analysis of the composite for treatment of dye wastewater.

The Fe3O4 nanoparticle is prepared by a green-inspired method using Musa paradisiaca L. (banana peel), having a high content of polyphenols and flavonoids, which reduces the metal precursors to form nanoparticles [21]. The waste banana peel is collected from fruit waste and washed with double distilled water (DDW) to remove any dust particles from the peels. Finally, shredded peels are boiled in 100 mL DDW until the solution’ color changed from transparent to yellow. The resulting solution is filtered and stored in a refrigerator for further experiments. In a typical process, FeCl3.6H2O (4 M) and CH3COONa (40 M) with 40 mL freshly prepared extract solution are ultra-sonicate for 1 h and then transferred to a Teflon autoclave reactor of 100 mL capacity. The mixture is subjected to heating at 200 °C for a duration of 5 h, followed by a gradual cooling process to reach room temperature. The dark precipitate is filtered out and then repeatedly rinsed with deionized water and ethanol before being dried in the air for 24 h at 70 °C. The schematic illustration of Fe3O4 synthesis is shown in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coating of carbon dots over Fe3O4 (BFOCD)
ce:section.ce:para:
Carbon dots are prepared from facile green hydrothermal method using apple peel as a carbon source. In a concise process, apple peels are cut into small fragments, dried in an oven at 80 °C, and subsequently ground. These ground peels are then blended with a mixture of DDW and C2H5OH in a 75:25 v/v ratio. The resulting mixture is placed in an autoclave reactor, heated at 180 °C for 5 h, and allowed to cool gradually to room temperature. The obtained dark brown solution is filtered through 0.45 μm filter to remove large particles. The reference taken for the measurement of quantum yield (QY) of CDs is quinine sulphate solution, which is prepared in 0.1 M H2SO4. The quantum yield of quinine sulphate was taken as 54 % at 360 nm wavelength [22]. The Eq. (1) is used for calculation of QY, % of CDs;

R represents absorbance value of CDs and quinine sulphate, and refractive index is represented by η . The refractive index of quinine sulphate was 1.33.

Finally, 0.3 g of prepared Fe3O4 nanoparticles is mixed with 15 mL of prepared CD solution and sonicated for 1 h to completely coat the dots on the nanoparticles. After sonication, the product is separated using an external magnet, the supernatant is decanted, and the carbon dot-coated iron oxide is washed with DDW and dried in an oven at 70 °C for characterization purpose.

SA and distilled water are combined to form a solution containing 1 % (w/v) SA. After mixing SA hydrogel, the prepared BFOCD solution (sonicated product of 15 mL CD with 0.3 g Fe3O4 as discuss in Section 2.2) is added slowly to provide homogeneity of nanoparticle and stirred for 12 h. The obtained product undergoes centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 7 min, followed by thorough washing with DDW and acetone. It is then kept in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 6 h. The schematic illustration of SA-FOCD synthesis is shown in Fig. 1.

RSM is a well-known approach for the demonstrating and optimizing the impacts of different input variables on the output of any process. It comprises the set of mathematical and statistical methods for the developing a model while reducing the number of experiments in the process to achieve effective results. In this experiment, three input variables are utilized for the determination of adsorption capacity of composite for adsorption of methyl violet (MV) dye using Box-Behnken design (BBD). BBD requires 3 level for each factor and are class of rotatable or nearly rotatable second order equations. This design requires fewer treatment combination than a central composite design (CCD). CCD designs offer accurate predictions across the entire design space, even necessitating factor settings that extend beyond the range of factors in the initial factorial part. The BBD consists of three segments, where each segment comprises two variables set at full two-level arrangement, while the third variable is kept at zero level. The total number of experimental points is often provided by 2 k(k-1) + C0, where k is the number of components and C0 is the number of centre points [23]. Design Expert delicate product (version, 7.1.3), used to create the exploratory plan for optimizing the adsorption performance of composite for the MV dye using BBD. The three input variables are used for BBD which includes adsorbent dosage (A), initial dye concentration (B), and contact time (C) with three levels each (−1,0, and +1) to determine the relationship between coded and real values as given in Eq. (2):

In this study, the multi-layer perceptron (MLP) has been used, which comprises of an input layer, an output layer, and one or more hidden or intermediate layers. ANN architectures with a feedforward back propagation algorithm and ‘tansig’ in the input layer and ‘purelin’ in the output layer has been used. The optimal number of neurons in the hidden layer is determined by iteratively testing different topologies to find the configuration that yields the lowest mean square error (MSE) and the highest correlation coefficient (R2). This is done to guarantee that forecasts deviated as little as possible from experimental data and reduces the challenge of over-fitting the model. To prevent the issues of overfitting and reduced convergence rate, excessive utilization of both large and small numbers of neurons has been avoided [25]. The data is divided into three sections training, validation, and testing as 70 %, 15 % and 15 %. Therefore 114 and 57 samples are employed for the training and testing subsets, respectively. Training data is used for determining ideal weights, testing used for calculating many epochs to minimise data overfitting, and validation is used for assessing the professional network's stability. All the experimental data set are normalised in between 0 and 1 using the Eq. (4).

To analyse the various parameters of the isotherm and kinetics investigation, both linear and non-linear approaches has been used. To achieve the best results for non-linear approaches, the Microsoft Excel Solver add-in function is employed. The chi-squared value (χ2) is determined for best fit model by computing with the Eq. Fig. 2(a) displays the FTIR analysis of banana peel extract and its synthesized magnetic nanoparticle. Strong and broad peaks within the 3400–3300 cm−1 range are attributed to -OH stretching in the phenols present within the banana peel extract. The peak at 2075 cm−1 in the banana peel extract signifies the existence of an aromatic compound with CH bending, while peaks at 1640 and 1580 cm−1 denote the vibration of carboxyl (C=O) and amide (-NH) groups, respectively [26]. Notably, the hydroxyl (-OH) peak in the BFO spectrum is slightly shifted to 3450 cm−1. Furthermore, a peak at 1630 cm−1 indicates the presence of biomolecules involved in the nanoparticle formation process. The characteristic peaks of FeO groups at 570 cm−1 confirm the formation of the magnetic nanoparticle. The possible mechanism for the formation of nanoparticles is that carbohydrates and polyphenols in banana peel extract act as a reducing agent [27]. At the same time, sodium acetate acts as an electrostatic stabilizing and capping agent for the formation of magnetic nanoparticles. Sodium acetate reacts with iron precursor and forms iron acetate, an intermediate complex phase of iron oxide acetate hydroxide hydrate (Eq. (10)). This complex further decomposed into oxide and water (Eq. (11)). When banana extract is added to both ferric and ferrous oxidation states solution, it forms Fe3O4 nanoparticles by interacting with carbohydrates present in the extract in an autoclave reactor. The carbohydrate is converted to gluconic acid, forming Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Eq. (12)).

As indicated in Fig. 2(b), SA spectra show stretching vibration of -OH at 3428 cm−1. Peaks occurring at 2926 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of aliphatic groups (-CH). Asymmetric vibrations of carboxylate groups (-COO) occur at 1610 cm−1. Additionally, peaks at 1080 and 1020 cm−1 correspond to the elongation of -CO groups in the pyranose ring and CO stretching, with contributions stemming from C–C–H and C–O–H deformation [28]. All these characteristics of SA are also observable after the incorporation of magnetic nanoparticles (SA-FO) and carbon dots (SA-FOCD). After incorporating magnetic nanoparticles, the spectrum of SA-FO shows a decrease in the intensity of all peaks compared to SA. However, there is an increase in the peak at 570 cm−1, indicating the presence of FeO groups in the biopolymer composite. After incorporating carbon dots over magnetic nanoparticles, the spectrum of SA-FOCD shows enhancement in peaks of 1610 cm−1, 1410 cm−1, 1080 cm−1, and 570 cm−1 compared to FO. It suggests that carbon dots-coated magnetic nanoparticles are successfully embedded in a solid matrix of sodium alginate. As per the literature, the nature of the interaction between nanofiller and alginate polymer can be determined by peaks of asymmetric and symmetric vibration of carboxylate groups (-COO) [29]. Determining the type of carboxylate coordination between nanoparticles and alginate polymer relies on the distinction in peaks between the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of -COO groups, as presented in Eq. (13) [30].

The XRD patterns of green synthesized magnetic nanoparticles are similar to those reported in our previous paper (synthesized by co-precipitation method) [16]. Fig. 2(c) shows XRD patterns with diffraction angle at 2θ = 30.3°, 35.5°, 57°, and 63.17° corresponding to miller indices (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440). These miller indices plane agrees with JCPDS Card No: 19–0629, which confirms the crystallographic structure of nanoparticles. By comparing JCPDS Card No:19-0629 [32], the synthesized nanoparticles are found to be magnetite (Fe3O4) but not maghemite (γ-Fe2O3). The estimated particle size of nanoparticles is 18.7 nm. The XRD patterns of SA with iron oxide nanoparticles show similar peaks but with less intensity, showing the composite's amorphous nature. The loss of crystallinity might be due to the coverage of sodium alginate over iron oxide nanoparticles. However, after the incorporation of CDs over nanoparticles, new peaks at 2θ = 26° have occurred, representing the presence of graphitic carbon (sp2) of CD. The rest of the XRD pattern of SA-FOCD is the same as that of SA-FO, attributed to the amorphous nature of the composite. The estimated particle sizes of SA-FO and SA-FOCD are 40.2 nm and 35.35 nm. VSM analysis has been performed for synthesized nanoparticle and SA composite, as shown in Fig. 2(d). It represents the magnetic hysteresis curve showing the ferromagnetism property of nanoparticle (BFO) and SA-FOCD with the saturation magnetization of 54.23 emu/g and 31.85 emu/g. A composite's magnetization property has been reduced compared to BFO because of the incorporation of SA and CDs, which do not retain any magnetic behavior.

The elemental states and surface functional groups of CDs are determined by XPS analysis. The three peaks at 283 eV, 301 eV, and 530 eV in the XPS survey scan correspond to C1s, N1s, and O1s, which shows that CDs have carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen with an atomic percentage of 78.41 %, 6.5 %, and 19.08 % (as shown in Fig. 3 (a)). The nitrogen content is higher than in the reported literature [33], contributing to surface passivation of CDs. The high-resolution spectra of C1s show three peaks (Fig. 3(b)): 281.8 eV corresponding to CC, 283.06 eV attributing to CN, and 286.09 eV assigning to CO bonds [34]. The high-resolution spectra of O1s show two peaks (Fig. 3(c)); 530 eV set to C-OH/C-O-C, and another small peak at 531 eV could be due to CO [33,35]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s can be resolved into two peaks (Fig. 3(d)): 396.83 eV corresponding to CN, and 398.76 eV attributing to CN [36].

The elemental valence of SA-FOCD has been characterized by XPS analysis, as shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS survey scan of composite, as shown in Fig. 4(a), shows four peaks which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Fe2p at 280 eV, 398 eV, 528 eV, and 708 eV of binding energies. Elements of carbon and nitrogen originate from SA and carbon dots. The C1s spectrum region of the composite is deconvoluted into three peaks with binding energies of 284.5, 286.8, and 288.8 eV, as shown in Fig. 4(b). These peaks feature sp2/sp3 bonded carbon of sodium alginate and CDs, CO arising from the moiety of the pyranose ring, β-(1–4)-glycosidic bonds of SA, and CO are attributed to carboxyl groups of biopolymer and dots. The studies in the past have described similar outcomes [37–39]. The high-resolution spectra of N1s resolved into two peaks (Fig. 4 (c)); 396.83 eV corresponding to CN, and 398.76 eV attributing to CN [36]. The O1s spectrum deconvoluted into three peaks at 528.59, 530.31, and 532.46 eV, which correspond to the O atom in the iron oxide (FeO), carbonyl group (CO) in biopolymer and carboxyl group (C=O) from biopolymer and dots as shown in Fig. 4(d). The Fe2p spectrum of the SA-FOCD composite deconvoluted into five peaks, as shown in Fig. 4(e). Peaks with binding energy 708.08 eV and 722 eV represent Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively. These broad peaks segregate into four at 707.76 eV, 722.66 eV of Fe2+, 712 eV, and 725 eV of Fe3+, respectively. Additionally, two peaks at 712.46 eV and 721.20 eV are satellite peaks of Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2, showing the purity and successful formation of the Fe3O4 phase. These results correlate pretty well with the literature [40,41].

The thermal stability of pristine SA, SA with FO, and SA with FOCD was performed by TGA/DTA analysis by heating nanocomposites under an N2 atmosphere, as shown in Fig. S1 (a, b). The TGA analysis of pristine SA and SA-FOCD reveals four stages of weight loss. The first stage for all the materials occurs between 20 and 230 °C (with weight loss of 0.49 mg, 0.75 mg, and 0.79 mg) due to loss of absorbed water. The second stage of SA occurs between 200 and 350 °C (weight loss is 2.49 mg), corresponding to fracture of glycosidic bonds. At the same time, the weight loss for SA-FOCD is lower than SA, which is 1.18 g. It might be because of CDs over FO nanoparticles, which show the thermal stability of the composite. However, the second stage for SA-FO results in complete weight loss due to the degradation and decomposition of SA polymer chains. The third stage of SA (350–700 °C) and SA-FOCD (320–520 °C) correspond to degradation of biopolymer with weight loss of 0.9 mg and 1.57 mg. After this stage, SA polymer shows complete degradation while SA-FOCD shows stability in weight till 800 °C. In the DTA curve of SA-FOCD, the second and third stages also display an exothermic peak; following this, no further temperature-associated decomposition is observed. These results confirm that incorporating CDs over iron oxide nanofiller shows better thermal stability than pristine SA and SA-FO. BET analysis measures the surface area and pore size of the SA-FOCD composite. The composite has micro and mesopores, according to the adsorption-desorption and type-IV isotherm. This leads to capillary condensation in the mesopores at the relative pressure range of 0.099 to 0.9. In this case, the desorption path differs from the adsorption path, resulting in multi-molecular layer adsorption and making a hysteresis loop, as shown in Fig. S1(c). This composite exhibits H3-type hysteresis and a small amount of H4-type hysteresis, which are the characteristics of wedge and slit-like pores with various distributions of pore size and pore diameter [42]. The desorption curve is above the adsorption curve, which may result from chemical interactions, expansion of non-rigid micropores, and irreversible adsorption of adsorbate molecules if the pores aperture is unaffected [43]. The BET surface area of the composite is 14.56 m2/g. BJH and DFT analyzed the pore size distribution plots of SA-FOCD. DFT captures the mechanisms of mesopore condensation, evaporation, hysteresis, and micropore filling. The NLDFT approach for pore size distribution, as shown in Fig. S1 (d), offers a total pore volume of 0.0342 cc/g and pore size of 14.85 Å (1.485 nm). Moreover, the BJH pore size distribution shows that the total volume is 0.0419 cc/g, and the pore size is 7.531 Å (0.753 nm), as shown in Fig. S1 (d). The traditional BJH approach is frequently employed for characterizing porosity. It produces precise findings for many meso- and macro-porous materials, although its accuracy is sometimes compromised. The BJH technique has a significant drawback: it cannot be used with microporous materials [44]. Therefore, precise pore size and pore volume of mesoporous (SA-FOCD) materials can be considered from the NLDFT approach.

Green route is employed to synthesize the magnetic nanoparticles, with banana extract as the reducing agent. The addition of extract into the Fe solution changed the color from greenish yellow to reddish brown after solvothermal treatment, demonstrating the production of BFO. An examination of the UV–Vis spectrum validates the production of BFO nanoparticles. The UV spectrum reveals absorption peaks at 200 nm and 370 nm. The peak at 200 nm shows the characteristic peak of Fe3O4 nanoparticles, and the surface plasmon peak of BFO occurs at about 390 nm, as shown in Fig. 6 (a). Similar outcomes in the green production of nanoparticles have been reported in the literature [45]. The UV–Visible analysis of CDs shows π-π* transition of CC at 280 nm and n-π* transition of CO at 310 nm [22] (Fig. S1(e)). The combination of CDs with BFO nanoparticles shows a similar peak in the UV spectrum. The shape of the spectrum of BFOCD is complicated due to the interaction of CD with BFO nanoparticles. It offers a new peak at 350 nm, which can be due to π- π* transition between carboxyl groups of CDs and BFO nanoparticles (Fig. 6 (b)). Further, incorporating BFOCD nanofiller in SA shows similar peaks but with higher absorbance than that of BFOCD, as shown in Fig. 6 (c). These results confirm the formation of the SA-FOCD composite. The Ebg values derived from extrapolating the linear sections of the Tauc curve for BFO, CDs, and BFOCD nanoparticles yield calculations of 4.16 eV, 3.51 eV, and 4.62 eV, respectively, for direct electronic transitions. In contrast, the SA-FOCD composite exhibits an energy band gap of 2.89 eV, which decreased the overall composite's energy band gap compared to nanofillers. It can be due to the interaction of BFOCD with sodium alginate biopolymer, which increases the molecular orbitals of BFOCD. It results in a decrease in the gap between the bonding and antibonding molecular orbitals. Another reason might be an increase in the size of composite particles, which reduces band gap energy.

The maximum peak or the PL intensity of CDs is observed at 470 nm when excited at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which is higher than the PL intensity of emission at an excitation wavelength of 280 nm. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were also studied at different excitation wavelengths, which suggests that the emission wavelength increases and shifts (460 nm to 485 nm) as the excitation wavelength increases from 270 nm to 380 nm, as shown in Fig. 5 (a). The availability of photo-induced electrons and holes at different energy levels in CDs can be responsible for shifting emission spectra [46]. The QY of CDs was found to be 23 % concerning quinine sulfate as a reference at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, which is higher than reported in the literature [47]. It is worth noting that apple peel primarily comprises carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and hydrogen elements. Therefore, this naturally sourced material is a potential precursor for synthesizing CDs. The CDs attributed the fluorescence-enhancing impact of nitrogen dopant atoms present on the surface of the CDs. This enhancement is convincingly demonstrated through the analysis of XPS results.

Moreover, a significant change in fluorescence intensity has been observed for 4 months of storage period (Fig. 5 (b)). However, no significant difference is observed in one month, confirming the CDs' notable photostability. The photostability of CDs was also investigated under various concentrations of NaCl (M) to understand the ionic strength tolerance of CDs (Fig. 5 (c)). It shows minute changes in the fluorescence intensity, which suggests that CDs have good stability and tolerance towards ionic strength. Subsequently, the effect of pH on CDs shows that maximum fluorescence intensity is occurring at pH 6 (Fig. 5 (d)). Fig. 5 (e). shows the fluorescence decay curve of CDs and SA-FOCD. Compared with CDs, the average fluorescence lifetime of SA-FOCDs was increased from 4.19 ns to 5.4 ns.

Analyzing various excitation wavelengths for the fluorescence spectra of BFOCD unveils an increase and shift in the emission wavelength (from 400 nm to 540 nm) as the excitation wavelength rises from 320 nm to 390 nm, as depicted in Fig. 6 (d). The outcomes suggest that the BFOCDs have crisp emission spectra that are excitation dependent. The emission spectra of BFOCDs reveal a prominent emission peak centered at 470 nm when excited at 360 nm. The most pronounced emission spectra are observed at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm, after which the spectrum shifts to higher excitation wavelengths. Emission spectra obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm further substantiate the formation of CDs coating over BFO, as demonstrated in Fig. 6 (e). Upon the addition of CDs to BFO nanoparticles, one observes fluorescence quenching. A potential explanation for this phenomenon is that the iron oxide nanoparticles are coated with carbon dots, as substantiated by the coordination between Fe3+ ions and the carbonyl groups of carbon dots. The hypothesis is that the excited state electrons of the carbonyl group transfer to the open d orbitals of the BFO material, leading to fluorescence quenching [48]. The emission spectra of SA-FOCD show the highest luminescence intensity compared to BFOCD and CD.

The broad emission has been observed over a range of 380 nm −540 nm for SA-FOCD at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The composite's emission intensity is reduced compared to CDs, as shown in Fig. 6(f). Under irradiation, electrons in CD are photoexcited, leaving positively charged holes in the valence band. Due to the instability of excited states without other materials, electrons will quickly transition to the valence band, emitting fluorescence. However, in the case of SA-FOCD, heterojunction arises at the interface of BFOCD and SA, where there is a charge separation area due to a change in the Fermi energy levels leading to a flow of electrons typically flow from the higher to lower Fermi levels [49,50]. Thus, better bonding between the materials must make it easier for electrons to transfer, reducing the emission spectra of SA-FOCD.

Fig. 7 (a) shows SEM images of magnetic nanoparticles (BFO) formed by green synthesis using banana peel extract. The surface morphology of the nanoparticles shows an uneven, spherical, and aggregating shape. The size of the nanoparticles from TEM results varied from 30 to 40 nm (Fig. 8 (a)). After the incorporation of CD with BFO, the dots form a coating over nanoparticles, which results in the reduction of particle size (19–35 nm) and agglomeration challenges (Fig. 8 (b)). SEM images of SA show a spherical shape like beads and smooth surface morphology (Fig. 7(b)). The dispersion of iron oxide nanoparticles in the biopolymer resulted in a porous structure, but the structure shows agglomerated nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 7(c). However, the dispersion of nanoparticles with dots on biopolymers forms a uniform, porous film-like structure, as shown in Fig. 8(d). EDX mapping of SA-FOCD can further support the findings mentioned above. The mapping of Fe, C, and O elements on the surface of the composite shows uniform dispersion with weight % of 18.43, 26.83, and 54.74, as shown in Fig. S3. The surface of the composite changes and pores of the adsorbent have been filled due to the adsorption of MV dye, as shown in Fig. 7 (e). The EDX analysis reveals an increase in the carbon (C) content (30.96 %) after adsorption due to MV dye. The reduction in iron (Fe) concentration (10.76 %) after dye adsorption suggests a possible ion exchange process between Fe3+ ions and dye molecules. TEM analysis of SA-FO and SA-FOCD shows average particle sizes are 37.5 and 31.3 nm (Fig. 8 (c-d)), which agrees with XRD results.
The effect of BFO, CDs, and SA concentration, dye solution pH, and dye adsorption temperature are available in the Supplementary file.

The RSM optimization model consists of several key steps. These steps include (1) selecting the response variable, (2) choosing the input variables and their respective levels, (3) designing the experimental plan for dye adsorption, (4) performing regression analysis, (5) analyzing the experimental results using ANOVA and developing a polynomial equation, (6) examining the effects of factors and their interactions through 3D surface and contour plots, (7) determining the optimal settings for the input variables, and (8) conducting a confirmation test to validate the optimization outcome. In this study, the adsorption performance of the composite has been investigated using the RSM with a BBD to analyse the influence of independent factors. A total 17 number of experiments were performed. Adsorbent dosage, initial dye concentration, and contact time are used as independent variables for the three parameters under consideration, and their individual and combined effects on the adsorption capacity of SA-FOCD for MV adsorption are explored using the BBD technique. The evaluation of the ANOVA test for the significance of the regression model for the response is shown in Table S1. Model terms are considered significant when “Prob > F” is <0.0500. Model terms are not significant if the value is higher than 0.05. A, B, C, AB, BC, A2, B2, A2B, and A2C are considerable model terms in this study. However, AC and C2 are not significant terms. The impact of each model parameter on the response value has been assessed. As a result, each component's percentage contribution (PC) is computed using the sum of squares from the ANOVA. The factor with a higher impact on the response will have a higher PC value. Thus, it is clear from Table S2 that adsorbent dosage and contact time collectively impact the adsorption performance of SA-FOCD.

A quadratic mathematical equation shows the relationship between input parameters, their interactions, and adsorption capacity. The coefficient of determination (R2) value is 99.1 %, which implies that 99 % variation in the adsorption capacity explains the variation in the input parameters. It indicates a good correlation between predicted and actual values, as shown in Fig. S5(a). The final equation in terms of actual factors is given by Eq. (14)
(14)

In Eq. (14), a positive sign signifies a synergistic interaction between the components, whereas a negative sign signifies an antagonistic interaction between the elements [51].

The interaction effect between three factors (AB and BC) significantly influences the adsorption capacity of the composite. The interaction between different factors can be present using 3D surface and 2D contour plots. In this plot, two factors change while one factor remains constant. The term AB, i.e., the interaction of adsorbent dosage (A) and initial dye concentration (B), is considered a significant term with a p-value of <0.0001, as given in Table S2. 3D plots and contour plots of the effect of the interaction of adsorbent dosage and initial dye concentration on the adsorption capacity of the composite are in Fig. 9 (a) and (b). The adsorption increases (from 33.92 mg/g to 352.38 mg/g) with an increase in initial dye concentration (10–100 ppm) and decreases with an increase in adsorbent dosage (0.01 to 0.03 g). It might be due to the overlapping or gathering of adsorption sites, which lowers the adsorbent surface area the dye can access and lengthens the diffusion path.

Additionally, as the adsorbent dosage increases, the time needed to attain equilibrium reduces. Previous studies have reported similar behavior [52,53]. Further, increasing dye concentration and a fixed amount of composite would cause more MV molecules to saturate the binding sites on the surface area. The adsorption rate would slow down because there would be fewer adsorption sites. The interaction between initial dye concentration (B) and contact time (C) is also consider to be significant, with a p-value of <0.0001. At pH 6, the adsorption capacity demonstrates an upward trend with an increase in dye concentration ranging from 10 to 100 ppm. This effect is attributed to an elevation in the concentration gradient between the solid and liquid phases. Further, an increase in contact time between adsorbent and dye improves the binding of dye molecules with active sites of an absorbent. However, at higher dye concentrations, the adsorbent reaches saturation after 60 min, resulting in less adsorption.

The optimum condition for the adsorption of MV dye is determined by numerical optimization in which each variable selects the desired goal. These goals are ‘minimizing the response’, ‘maximizing the response’, ‘target within a specific range’, and ‘no one’. These goals use all the parameters and responses to build desirability indices. It displays the preferred intervals for each target (di). The optimum ranges run from 0 to 1 (correspondingly, least desired to most desirable). The overall desirability function D is calculated as the geometric average of the individual desirability functions of each response di (Yi) where n is the number of responses (Eq. (15)) [54].
(15)
D =
∏
i = 1
n
d i
Y i
1 n

Since the main goal of this study is to maximize composite adsorption performance, the adsorbent dosage is chosen as ‘minimum,’ initial dye concentration is ‘within range,’ contact time is ‘high,’ and adsorption capacity is ‘maximum.’ The weight assigned to each factor and response are given in Table S3. The weight also indicates the significance based on upper and lower boundaries. The importance of all factors and responses is also depicted in Table S3. Based on the numerical optimization tool, the maximum adsorption capacity of the composite is 232.44 mg/g with an adsorbent dosage of 0.02, initial dye concentration of 100 ppm, and contact time of 60 min with a desirability value of 0.820.

Additional experiments are performed at optimal conditions to validate the precited results. The average adsorption capacity is determined by repeating experiments twice. The experimental adsorption capacity is found to be 199.63 mg/g. The predicted and experimental values of the adsorption capacity of SA-FOCD are in good agreement (Table 2 ), which implies validation of the optimization result.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Prediction by ANN model and comparison by RSM model
ce:section.ce:para: The input parameters are the same as in Table 1, and the output parameter is adsorption capacity. The feedforward backpropagation network is trained with 4 layers (1 input layer, 2 hidden layers, and 1 output layer) and 5 neurons in each hidden layer. The best validation performance is achieved at epoch 146 with an MSE of 1.46. The parameters of the trained ANN are examined using the testing data set with ±4 error. The R2 and MSE values of different numbers of neurons in the hidden layer for both training and testing data sets of output variables have been compared. The ANN model with two hidden layers (5 neurons) makes the least mean square error and a high coefficient of determination using a testing data set. Therefore, this structure was applied to design the ANN model. The best topology observed for the 3 input parameters and 1 output response comes out to be 3–5–5-1. To examine the effectiveness of the constructed ANN and RSM models for predicting the adsorption capacity of SA-FOCD for MV, the corresponding R2, ARE, MSE, and RMSE values are used. R2, ARE, MSE, and RMSE values for the RSM model are found to be 0.9913, 0.124 %, 6.689, and 2.586, respectively. However, the R2, ARE, MSE, and RMSE values for the created optimum ANN model are 0.999, 0.623 %, 2.923, and 1.710, respectively. Both models might be used to forecast the experimental data based on their extremely high R2 values. However, when comparing ARE, MSE, and RMSE values, the ANN model has demonstrated stronger predictive performance than the RSM model. Furthermore, in Fig. 10 , the residuals of both the RSM and ANN predicted models are depicted for each experimental run. The figure demonstrates that the trained neural network model exhibited less deviation in estimating the experimental values than the RSM model. Specifically, the ANN model displayed lower residual values ranging from +3.09 to −1.19, while the RSM model had residual values ranging from +3.76 to −3.76. On the other hand, the created RSM model can simplify the issue by expressing the significant/insignificant model terms in terms of a regression equation and elucidating the simultaneous interaction effect of process factors on the adsorption capacity of SA-FOCD. Unlike RSM, which can only capture quadratic approximations, ANN can approximate a wide range of non-linear polynomials, contributing to its superior prediction ability [14].

The Freundlich model best fits with R2 of 0.99 and minimum χ2 as 12.46, as shown in Fig. S6 (a). According to the data above, MV typically exhibited multilayer adsorption instead of monolayer adsorption in its adsorption behavior. Additionally, it is apparent from Table S4 that the value of 1/n is smaller than 1, indicating that the adsorption behavior of MV on SA-FOCD is favorable. Compared to a pseudo-1st-order model with an R2 value of 0.99, the pseudo-2nd-order model best fits the kinetic model (Fig. S6 (b,c)). Additionally, the computed data and actual data for the adsorption of MV agreed well, as shown in Fig. S6 (d). However, chemical adsorption is assumed to determine the rate-limiting step when the pseudo-2nd-order kinetic model is established. Some studies showed that the rate-limiting phase in chemisorption involves valence force through electron sharing or exchange [53]. As a result, these findings support the EDX analysis results that MV adsorption occurs via ion exchange.

Moreover, the IPD model also shows higher regression coefficients for all concentrations of dyes. This model has three main steps; (I) external diffusion or boundary layer diffusion; diffusion of solute through boundary layer between adsorbate and adsorbent (II) internal diffusion or pore film diffusion; diffusion of solute into pores of adsorbent, and (III) equilibrium stage; interaction of adsorbate with adsorbent chemically or physically where intra-particle diffusion slows down [55]. Step (I)- Step (III) are generally affected by adsorbent properties such as external surface area, surface reactivity, thickness of boundary layer, pore structure, and pore size of an adsorbent. For the adsorption of MV onto SA-FOCD, the IPD rate constant, Kid, decreased while the intercept, C, signifying the boundary layer effect, has a higher value for step (II) and step (III) as compared to step (I) for initial dye concentration of 10–80 ppm Table S5. The lower rate constants in the second and third steps represent slower adsorption, which means the achievement of equilibrium. However, the intercept or boundary layer thickness for 100 ppm and 80 ppm shows a negative value, possibly due to the complex adsorption process and boundary layer impeding the IPD of high concentrations of dyes. Two linear graphs have been observed for concentrations ranging from 10 ppm to 60 ppm, whereas three linear graphs can be seen for 70 ppm, 80 ppm, and 100 ppm. Since Fig. S6 (e) contains two linear graphs for lower concentrations, equilibrium adsorption, and boundary layer diffusion may partially regulate adsorption at lower starting dye concentrations. However, with larger dye concentrations, the adsorbent's micropores, mesopores, and macropores also play a role in the diffusion. Eq. (16) determines the diffusion coefficient. To understand whether IPD is the rate-limiting step [56].

where Di is the diffusion coefficient if Di value is between 10−5 and 10−13, then the rate-limiting step is intraparticle diffusion, r is the composite particle size (cm), and t0.5 is the time required to complete half adsorption. The value of Di comes out to be 1.21 × 10−13, which is in the range of 10−5 to 10−13, meaning that IPD is involved. Further, the kinetic behavior of a diffusion-based process based on intra-particle diffusion determine by the initial adsorption factor (Ri), initial adsorption amount (qt) at t = t0.5, and final adsorption amount (qr) at t = tr (longest time in adsorption) [56]. In this study, the value of Ri comes out to be 0.17, which lies in zone 3 means strong initial adsorption. These results suggest that surface adsorption predominated due to the high surface area, porous structure, and stronger reactivity of magnetite-based nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical modelling for adsorption of MV dye
ce:section.ce:para:
Traditional adsorption isotherms models like Langmuir and Freundlich can yield trustworthy findings for maximal adsorption capacity and affinity. However, these models do not offer crucial information for describing the adsorption mechanisms or pertinent insights into the adsorption dynamics. The dye adsorption process using statistical physical models has been performed to bridge this knowledge gap. To understand the adsorption of the MV molecule on SA-FOCD, we perform non-linear curve fitting on experimental data at various temperatures. According to [57], the monolayer adsorption and double layer adsorption can be described by Eq. (18) and Eq. (19)

where, C1/2 represnts the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent, Ce is equlibirum concentration, C1 is the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent in first layer, C2 is the concentration of dye solution at half saturated adsorbent in second layer, nD is the number of MV molecules bonded to adsorption sites, and Dm is the density of funtional groups. From adsorption isotherm results it is found that multilyaer adsorption is predominated with high R2 value in comparison to monolayer adsorption. In order to analyse the adsorption mechanism of dye, the double layer adsorption model is utilized in this work. The relevant parameters are given in Table 3 and non linear curve is shown in Fig. 11 .

By examining the values of nD derived from the fitting of experimental data, it has been shown in earlier studies that this parameter can characterize the orientation of adsorbates on an adsorbent surface throughout the adsorption process and the effect of temperature on the adsorbent surface [58]. According to the values of “nD” the literature has shown that there are three scenarios that might represent the adsorption orientation:
i.
nD < 0 suggested a parallel adsorption direction means that the functional group of an adsorbent can accept a portion of the dye molecules (i.e., sharing occurs by two or more functional groups). As a result, the adsorption process involves multi interactions.
ii.
0.5 < nD < 1 means the dye molecules can be adsorbed in two distinct percentages simultaneously through both parallel and non-parallel directions (i.e., mixed orientations).
iii.
nD > 1 means adsorption of MV molecules occurring as non-parallel, and one adsorption site can take various dye molecules and form a multimolecular layer

In this study, the value of nD at all temperatures is between 0.5 and 1, which suggests that mixed orientation, either parallel or non-parallel, occurs during the adsorption of MV on SA-FOCD. This observation implied that the relevant adsorption active site had successfully caught some adsorbate while anticipating a mixed orientation. A formula can determine the parallel to non-parallel orientation ratio, i.e., nD = P + (1 − P) 0.5. P denotes the fraction of non-parallel orientation in the formula, whereas 1-P represents the proportion of parallel orientation. Thus, 18 %, 15.6 %, and 0.8 % of MV molecules are in a non-parallel orientation, while 82 %, 84.4 %, and 99.2 %, respectively, are in parallel orientation at different temperatures.

Qsat is also an important factor in determining the contribution of adsorbate in adsorption process which is affected by several factors including temperatures. The expression of this parameter is Qsat = nD.Dm. The value of Qsat decreases from 467.57 to 348.37 with an increase in temperature from 40 to 60 °C. Because of the dissipation of the binding forces on the surface of the SA-FOCD adsorbent at higher temperatures, a reduction occurs in adsorption capacity. Additionally, the migration of dye molecules onto active adsorbent sites reduces. The adsorption energy computes to comprehend the adsorption process of MV on SA-FOCD. By measuring concentration at half-saturation, adsorption energy is determined by Eq. (20).
(20)
∆ E = RTln

R is universal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), T is temperature in K, Cs is the dye solubility. The calculated activation energy of the MV adsorption is given in Table 3. All the value of activation energy are positive which suggests that adsorption process is endothermic in nature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective adsorption of dyes and adsorption mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the aforementioned findings, the ternary composite SA-FOCD exhibits promising potential as an adsorbent for efficiently removing MV dye. This section investigates the adsorption efficiency of various other dyes, encompassing cationic and anionic dyes—three types of dyes are studied, namely, RhB, MR, and MV. The adsorption efficiency of dyes is 92 %, 93 %, and 99 % using 0.02 g of adsorbent at pH 6 with an initial dye concentration of 20 ppm, as shown in Fig. 12 (a). The SA-FOCD offers higher adsorption capability towards MV dye among all the dyes. The examination of SEM images indicated that SA-FOCD pores are visibly occupied following the adsorption of the dye. This observation suggests that pore filling plays a significant role in adsorption. As per the literature, the molecular size of MV is 1.4 nm [59]. The BET and DFT results show that the pores radius of the SA-FOCD composite is about 1.485 nm, comparable to the molecular size of MV dye. The absorbent consists of nanoscale pores; the outer and inner surfaces cover functional groups or active sites. As a result, MV shows higher adsorption efficiency than other dyes because it is narrow inside the pores and absorbed on the surface of an adsorbent. Furthermore, FTIR results show that the frequency of -COO and -OH peaks at 1610 cm−1-1410 cm−1 and 3400 cm−1 increases after adsorption of dye Fig. 12 (b). The results indicate interactions between the benzene ring in the dye molecule and the conjugated structure and between the -OH and -COO functional groups in SA-FOCD. Specifically, these interactions involve π-π interactions and n-π interactions. Likewise, the reduction in peaks of FeO groups confirms the ion exchange between dye molecules and the SA-FOCD composite. Moreover, the zeta potential results suggest that electrostatic attraction occurs between dye and SA-FOCD. The reason for different types of adsorption mechanisms would be due to the mixed-orientation (parallel or non-parallel) of the adsorption of MV on SA-FOCD. A schematic diagram of a possible mechanism for the adsorption of MV on SA-FOCD is shown in Fig. 12 (c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Column adsorption and regeneration of SA-FOCD
ce:section.ce:para:
The breakthrough curves from the MV sorption process are used to evaluate the effectiveness of the alginate-based adsorbents in the column investigations. The column experiment involves passing 20 ppm (200 mL) of MV dye solution through 1 g of adsorbents with a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min at pH 6. Fig. 13 (a) displays the breakthrough curves of the alginate-based adsorbents for removing MV dye. As shown in Fig. 13 (a), the resulting breakthrough curves resemble the typical S-shaped profile of continuous adsorption systems. The saturation of adsorbent is achieved in 1000–1200 min. The column adsorption has yet to be studied for higher flow rates because as the flow rate increases, the adsorbent bed height increases. This increase in bed height occurs due to the swelling behavior of the SA composite. However, the swelling behavior of SA is not noticed in the batch adsorption study. It can be due to the inclusion of D-mannuronic acid (pKa = 3.38) and L-guluronic acid units (pKa = 3.65) in the SA and pH below 7. In contrast, in the column study, the pH becomes 7 due to the continuous addition of water, and hence, the SA-FOCD swells due to the uptake of water between 7 and 12 [60]. We should maintain the pH in the column process to avoid material swelling and get better results. The breakthrough profile was analyzed by various parameters as described by Eq. (21) to Eq. Adsorption columns with high recycling and regeneration capabilities can extend their useful lives and lower the cost of treating dye effluent. The 0.01 M NaOH solution is passed through the column for dye desorption. Fig. 13 (b) demonstrated effective adsorption in further adsorption cycles and showed stable cyclic performance with a 0.2 mL/min flowrate.

The equations used for cost analysis of SA-FOCD are provided in our recent review paper [1]. All those equations are also mentioned in supplementary file. Fig. 13 (c) illustrates the expenses associated with treating 1 L of dye across various reuse cycles. These expenses are determined using the equations detailed in our earlier research publication [11]. The calculations encompass the cost per liter (C/L) of the absorbent, the purchase cost of the absorbent, desorption expenses, incineration charges, and distillation costs, as outlined in the supplementary file. The cost of generating 1 g of composite includes sodium alginate (0.0155 $), iron oxide nanoparticle (0.014$), sodium acetate (7.2 ×
10
− 5
$), HCl, ethanol (0.099$), and apple peel (1.6 ×
10
− 6
$).

In economies like India, it's usual to observe that the average EBITDA margin for prominent textile companies typically spans from 10 % to 27 % [62]. Given the lower profitability in the textile sector, literature suggests that these industries are willing to invest in contaminant removal only if the additional expenditure remains within the range of $4 to $6 per cubic meter of wastewater [63]. In our current investigation, the total cost per liter is found to be 5.7 ×
10
− 3
$/L, underscoring the significant potential of SA-FOCD for effective contaminant removal and water purification across various industries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of SA-FOCD with other adsorbents
ce:section.ce:para: The Table 4 lists different types of adsorbents, including the use of a sodium alginate-based composite for removal of dyes and metal ions from synthetic wastewater. Compared with other material systems, the SA-FOCD adsorbent maintains the largest adsorption capacity and exhibits good reusability. Additionally, this study includes an ANN model with a 3-5-5-2 configuration that demonstrates good predictivity and accuracy. This study also stands out from other reports by conducting a cost analysis of the adsorbent for treating synthetic dye wastewater. None of the literature listed in the Table 4 has performed such cost assessment, limiting their usability in large-scale applications for wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption performance of SA-FOCD for heavy metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the capability of SA-FOCD to adsorb heavy metal ions such as lead (Pb (II)), Cd(II), and Cr(VI) are also examined. The selectivity of the composite for metal ions is assessed by investigating the impacts of single, binary, and tertiary systems. The selectivity of the composite towards metal ions has been evaluated by studying the effects of single, binary, and tertiary systems. The experimental adsorption parameters are maintained the same as those identified as optimal for dye adsorption. The FTIR analysis is performed to investigate the mechanism of metal ion adsorption onto the PNCs. In the case of the single system, SA-FOCD exhibits the greatest adsorption capacity for Cr (VI), Pb (II), and Cd (II). The phenomenon ascribes to the electrostatic interaction between the surface charge and the dissolved ions, thereby influencing the surface complexation process. The protonation of surface groups has a more pronounced impact on Pb (II) and Cd (II) due to their lower charge densities and larger ionic radii. Consequently, this protonation leads to a reduction in the number of accessible adsorption sites on SA-FOCD. This effect is notable in the single system, where Pb(II) has an ionic radius of 1.2 Å and Cd(II) has an ionic radius of 0.97 Å [67]) in single system. Most studies on the competitive adsorption of metals have shown that the adsorption of metal ions in multi-metal ion systems is generally lower compared to a system with single metal ions of the same type. To examine this phenomenon, the competitive interaction among Cr (VI), Pb (II), and Cd (II) ions for the binding sites on the composite has been analyzed. When these metal ions coexist in a ternary mixture, the adsorption capacity of Cd (II) and Pb (II) is comparatively lower than that of Cr (VI). The sequence in which these metal ions are introduced during the adsorption process could impact the adsorption capacity of the composite. This consideration arises due to the finite number of available sites on the composite and the rivalry among the metal ions to occupy these sites in a multi-element adsorption scenario. Based on the information provided in Table S6, the Cr (VI) has a lower charge density as compared to Cd (II) and Pb (II), resulting in the highest adsorption capacity in the multi-metal ion mixture for all the composites. Charge density refers to an ion's charge ratio to its volume or ionic radius. In this case, the charge density of the ions influenced by the adsorption behavior. Simultaneous adsorption experiments involving Cr (VI), Pb (II), and Cd (II) indicated a selective sorption sequence as Cr (VI) > Pb (II) > Cd (II).

Fig. S7 displays the FTIR analysis of the metal ions after the adsorption process, revealing increased peaks at 2926 cm−1, 1610 cm−1, and 570 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of aliphatic groups (-CH), the stretching vibration of CC, and FeO groups. This suggests a stronger interaction between the adsorbent and the metal ions, potentially involving electrostatic attraction or redox processes [68]. Notably, a distinct band at 570 cm−1, likely attributed to Cd (II)/Pb(II)/Cr(VI)-O, is evident from spectra after adsorption, indicating the enrichment of metal ion species in the sample.

In summary, controlling morphologies, particle size, and adsorption performance of composite materials through tuning nanofiller properties is an area of great interest with many challenges. By employing precise synthesis techniques, surface functionalization, and hybridization with other materials, researchers can make significant strides in achieving the desired properties for various applications. This study successfully incorporates iron oxide nanoparticles into SA via carbon dot modification, forming a biodegradable, low-cost ternary composite with excellent stability. The SEM and EDX mapping results show that the dispersion of nanoparticles with dots on biopolymers forms a uniform, porous film-like structure. The TEM results show the reduction in the size of magnetic nanoparticles after incorporating CDs, which provides good dispersion over the biopolymer matrix. Further FTIR results confirm the formation of bidentate bridging coordination between CD-modified nanoparticles and SA. Fluorescence analysis shows the successful formation of CD coating due to fluorescence quenching mechanisms. The composite exhibits favorable properties for dye and metal ions removal from aqueous solutions, verified through FTIR, XRD, XPS, and UV–Vis/PL spectra. SEM/TEM and EDX analysis confirms uniform nanoparticle dispersion, which increases the interfacial reaction between polymer and nanofiller and consequently improves the adsorption performance. Optimal adsorption conditions for SA-FOCD towards MV dye have been determined using RSM with BBD design. The composite achieved optimal adsorption capacity under pH 6, 0.02 adsorbent dosage, 100 ppm initial dye concentration, and 60 min contact time. The comparison between ANN and RSM models shows that the ANN model has superior predictive ability, while the RSM model is more suitable for optimizing adsorption. The findings of the statistical physical model analysis showed that the adsorption of MV follows parallel and non-parallel orientation and shows an adsorption capacity of 467.57 mg/g. FTIR results proved that dyes and metal ions form an electrostatic attraction, π-π interaction, and hydrogen bonding with SA-FOCD. Column adsorption studies discovered that the maximum bed adsorption capacity for SA-FOCD is 198.6 mg/g for 30 ppm and can be recycled for 4–5 adsorption-desorption cycles. The synthesized composite offers economic feasibility with substantially reduced treatment costs and, thus, holds the potential for being employed in industries for water purification and removing contaminants.

I, Momina, as the corresponding author of the revised manuscript titled “Synthesis of Biodegradable Sodium Alginate-Based Carbon Dot-Nanomagnetic Composite (SA-FOCD) for Enhanced Water Remediation using ANN Modelling, RSM Optimization, and Economic Analysis” for publication in your esteemed journal “International Journal of Biological Macromolecules” hereby submit the revised version in response to the valuable feedback received during the peer review process.

We appreciate the valuable comments and suggestions from the reviewers, and we have thoroughly addressed each point raised. The revisions made are aimed at enhancing the clarity, accuracy, and overall quality of the manuscript.

All co-authors have reviewed and approved the changes made in this revised version. The data presented in the manuscript are accurate, and we have provided appropriate citations to the work of others.

We confirm that this manuscript has not been published previously and is not under consideration for publication elsewhere. We have checked that no intentional or unintentional plagiarism has occurred in the present manuscript.

As the corresponding author, I take responsibility for communication with the journal during the review process. If accepted, I will ensure that all co-authors are informed and agree to the final version for publication.
Thank you for the opportunity to revise and resubmit our manuscript. We look forward to the possibility of seeing our work published in International Journal of Biological Macromolecules.
